---
title: "Verifying AI Output"
---

::: {.topic-badges}
[Novice]{.level-badge .level-novice} · **Governance, Ethics & Equity** track · ~1 hour, self-paced
:::

[← Back to the Governance, Ethics & Equity track](governance-ethics.qmd){.track-back}

*Build the habit that makes everything else safe: checking AI output before it counts.*

## What you'll be able to do

- Spot the kinds of errors AI makes confidently
- Run a quick verification workflow on facts and citations
- Decide what level of checking a task needs

## Overview

AI states wrong facts, numbers, and citations with full confidence. Verification is the single habit that makes AI safe to use: check anything that will inform a real decision or document against the source.

Right-size the effort. A throwaway brainstorm needs little checking; a number in a report or a cited claim needs full verification. The rule: if you can't open it, you can't cite it.

## Practice activities

```{=html}
<details class="activity"><summary>Activity 1 · Novice — Catch a hallucination</summary>
```

**Time** ~15 min · **Tools** ChatGPT Edu

**Goal.** See how confidently AI invents sources, and build the checking habit.

**Setup.** A topic you know well.

**Steps.**

1. Ask for citations:

   > Give me five citations (authors, year, journal, title) on [topic].

2. Try to open and confirm each one (Google Scholar or the journal site).

3. Count how many were real, mis-attributed, or invented.

**Expected result.** A tally showing some are wrong — vivid, first-hand proof.

**Check your work.** A formatted citation is not a real one. If you can’t open it, you can’t cite it.

**Common pitfalls.** The confident, plausible errors are the dangerous ones. Verify before anything counts.

**Stretch (optional).** Ask for a statistic with a source and trace the number to the original.

```{=html}
</details>
```

```{=html}
<details class="activity"><summary>Activity 2 · Novice — Verify a number under time pressure</summary>
```

**Time** ~12 min · **Tools** ChatGPT Edu

**Goal.** Build a quick verification routine you’ll actually use.

**Setup.** A claim with a statistic (from AI or a draft).

**Steps.**

1. Ask for a sourced figure:

   > State a public-health statistic about [topic] and give the original source.

2. Trace the number to the primary source and confirm it — exact figure, year, population.

3. Ask yourself: would you have caught this if you were rushing?

**Expected result.** A verified (or debunked) statistic.

**Check your work.** Match effort to stakes — a number going into a report gets full verification.

**Common pitfalls.** Secondary sources drift from primaries; go to the original.

**Stretch (optional).** Write a 3-step personal “verify before it counts” checklist.

```{=html}
</details>
```


## Check your readiness

Answer these, then check — your score suggests whether to dive in or skim the readings first.

<div class="readiness-quiz">
<script type="application/json" class="quiz-data">
{"questions": [{"q": "The core rule for citations is…", "options": ["If you can't open it, you can't cite it", "Trust formatted citations", "Cite freely", "Skip citations"], "answer": 0, "explain": "Verify the source actually exists and says what's claimed."}, {"q": "How much verification does a task need?", "options": ["Scaled to the stakes", "Always maximum", "Always none", "Based on length"], "answer": 0, "explain": "Match effort to consequences."}, {"q": "AI errors are dangerous mainly because they're…", "options": ["Stated confidently and look right", "Always obvious", "Rare", "Flagged automatically"], "answer": 0, "explain": "Confident, plausible errors are the ones that slip through."}]}
</script>
</div>

## Recommended readings

Available in the shared OneDrive folder **Staff Faculty AI Workshop → Readings**, and online where linked:

- [**A Court Has Ruled Google Is Liable for False Statements Generated by AI**](https://www.wired.com/story/a-court-has-ruled-that-google-is-liable-for-false-statements-generated-by-ai-overviews/) — real accountability for AI errors.
- [**Results May Vary (Lance Eaton)**](https://aiedusimplified.substack.com/p/useful-but) — why the same prompt gives different answers — and what to do.

## Useful resources

- [ChatGPT Edu (Drexel AI Tools)](https://drexel.edu/provost/ai/tools) — the approved tool, with data rules.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Data rules
Verification applies to **all** outputs; never publish an AI-supplied fact or citation unchecked. See [Tools & Access](../tools.qmd) and Drexel's [AI Tools page](https://drexel.edu/provost/ai/tools).
:::
